steps.title: Full Family Tree
steps.desc: Full family vocabulary from grandparents down. Every word is a compound you can decode. Korean keeps VERY precise about family roles — every position has its own word.
goals: Learn full family tree
Decompose grandparent compound words
Master formal vs casual split

steps.note:
COMPOUND: 할 (grand) + 아버지 (father).
'Grand-father' = grandfather.

할 is a prefix meaning 'grand' — used only for grandparents.
건강하세요: honorific 'is healthy' (you use this for elders).

COMPOUND: 할 (grand) + 머니 (mother form).
Same 할 prefix as 할아버지.

잘 해요: does well.
잘: well/good. 잘 먹어요 (eats well), 잘 자요 (sleeps well).

steps.exampleEn: My father is an office worker.
steps.note:
아버지: formal (father).
아빠: casual (dad).

Use 아버지 when talking ABOUT someone's father.
Use 아빠 casually about your own dad.

우리 아빠 (my dad) — note 우리 not 나의.

어머니: formal (mother).
엄마: casual (mom).

Same formal/casual pattern as 아버지/아빠.

어머니, 아버지 = respectful
엄마, 아빠 = intimate

COMPOUNDS:
남 (male) + 동생 (younger sibling)
여 (female) + 동생 (younger sibling)

Good news: younger sibling terms do NOT change based on speaker gender.
남동생 = younger brother, said by anyone.
여동생 = younger sister, said by anyone.

남편: husband.
아내: wife.

남: male. 편: side/companion.
'Male companion' = husband.

부인 (부인): formal/honorific wife term.
와이프: Konglish casual (wife).

steps.hint: 할 = grand prefix, same as 할머니

steps.q: When talking to your friend about their father (formal), you say:
steps.hint: 아버지 = formal, 아빠 = casual for your own dad
남 (male) / 여 (female) — the core gender pair in Korean

steps.q: Which sibling term changes based on the SPEAKER's gender?
steps.hint: Older sibling terms depend on who is speaking
할 (grand) + 머니 (mother) = grandmother

steps.s: 저는 여동생이 ___ 있어요. (I have 1 younger sister — use counter)
steps.hint: People counter: 명. Native number 하나→한 before counter
My father is a doctor. My mother is a teacher.
남편 (husband) ↔ 아내 (wife)
Koreans say 우리 (our) even for individual family members

steps.title: Speaker Gender Determines the Word
steps.desc: You previewed this in U2. Now you own it. Korean encodes the SPEAKER's relationship into the word itself. Older sibling terms depend entirely on whether YOU are male or female.
goals: Master all four older sibling terms
Understand why the speaker's gender matters
Use these terms outside the family too

steps.exampleEn: I have an older brother. (said by a male)
steps.note:
형: used by MALE speakers for their older brother.

형! — very common address term between brothers.
Also used for older male friends (male speaker).

steps.exampleEn: My older brother is cool. (said by a female)
steps.note:
오빠: used by FEMALE speakers for their older brother.

Also used for:
• An older boyfriend
• A close older male friend (female speaker)
• Female fans calling male K-pop idols

오빠!: The most common word shouted at K-pop concerts.

steps.exampleEn: My older sister is pretty. (said by a male)
steps.note:
누나: used by MALE speakers for their older sister.

Also used for older female friends (male speaker).
누나! — affectionate address term.

steps.exampleEn: My older sister cooks well. (said by a female)
steps.note:
언니: used by FEMALE speakers for their older sister.

Also used for older female friends (female speaker).

언니! — what women call their older female friends,
older female colleagues, even familiar older customers.

steps.title: 🎯 The Complete Older Sibling Grid
steps.text:
The four words for 'older sibling' organized by
SPEAKER gender × SIBLING gender:

         | older BROTHER | older SISTER
---------+---------------+-------------
YOU male | 형            | 누나
YOU female | 오빠         | 언니

Memory trick:
• As a MALE: 형/누나 (both start rough-sounding)
• As a FEMALE: 오빠/언니 (both flow softly)

Beyond family:
These terms extend to close friends, colleagues,
and even strangers you feel close to.
형, 오빠, 누나, 언니 = 'building 정 (deep bond)'

steps.q: A female says '오빠가 있어요.' She has:
steps.hint: 오빠 = older brother, said by FEMALE speakers

steps.q: A male wants to say 'I have an older sister':
steps.hint: MALE speaker + older sister = 누나

steps.q: Female fans at a K-pop concert shout:
steps.hint: Female fans → male idol = 오빠

steps.q: Two male friends. The younger one addresses the older one as:
steps.hint: Male speaker + older male friend = 형

steps.s: 저는 남자예요. ___ 이 있어요. (I have an older sister)
steps.hint: Male speaker + older sister = 누나

steps.s: 저는 여자예요. ___ 가 있어요. (I have an older brother)
steps.hint: Female speaker + older brother = 오빠

steps.s: 저는 여자예요. ___ 가 있어요. (I have an older sister)
steps.hint: Female speaker + older sister = 언니
Female speaker: I have an older brother AND an older sister

steps.q: These sibling terms can be used for:
steps.hint: Korean social bonds extend family terms to close relationships

steps.title: My House = Our House
steps.desc: Koreans say 우리 (our) where English says 'my' for family and close things. This isn't a mistake — it reflects Korean collectivism. Plus: the possessive particle 의.
goals: Use 우리 for family and close possessions
Use 의 as a possessive marker
Understand Korean collectivism through language

steps.en: our / my (for family and close things)
steps.note:
우리: 'our' — but used where English says 'my':

우리 엄마: my mom
우리 집: my house
우리 나라: my/our country
우리 학교: my/our school
우리 회사: my/our company

나의 엄마 sounds unnatural in Korean.
Use 우리 엄마 instead.

의: possessive particle.

엄마의 이름: mom's name
한국의 음식: Korea's food
아버지의 직업: father's job

의 is often dropped in casual speech:
엄마 이름 (without 의) is equally natural.

제: formal/polite 'my' (저 → 제 + 의).
내: casual 'my' (나 → 내 + 의).

제 이름: my name (polite)
내 이름: my name (casual)

우리 is used for family.
제/내 is used for personal items, names, etc.

steps.title: 🏠 우리 집 vs 내 방: Korean Collectivism
steps.text:
Korean collectivism shows in pronouns:

우리 (our → my) is used for:
• Family members: 우리 엄마, 우리 아빠
• Home: 우리 집, 우리 동네 (our neighborhood)
• School/work: 우리 학교, 우리 회사
• Country: 우리 나라 (South Korea)

제/내 (my) is used for:
• Personal items: 제 가방, 내 책
• Characteristics: 제 이름, 내 생각

The logic: family and community are SHARED.
'My house' belongs to everyone in it.
'My mom' raised the whole family.
So 우리 is more natural.

나의 엄마 sounds cold, even selfish.

steps.hint: Family = 우리, not 나의 or 저의
엄마의 이름 = mom's name
나라 = country. Koreans say 우리 나라 to mean South Korea.
Personal items use 제 (polite) or 내 (casual), not 우리

steps.s: ___ 아버지는 의사예요. (My father is a doctor)
steps.hint: Family member → use 우리

steps.s: 이것은 ___ 가방이에요. (This is my bag — polite)
steps.hint: Personal item, polite context → 제

steps.s: 한국의 ___ 이/가 유명해요. (Korea's food is famous)
steps.hint: 한국의 음식 = Korea's food

steps.q: 의 in casual speech is often:
steps.hint: 엄마 이름 (without 의) sounds perfectly natural

steps.title: 크다 = to be big. 작다 = to be small.
steps.desc: English separates adjectives (big) from verbs (is). Korean doesn't. 크다 means BOTH 'big' AND 'to be big.' They conjugate exactly like action verbs. This is one of the most important Korean grammar facts.
goals: Understand descriptive verbs
Conjugate크다, 작다, 예쁘다 in present polite
See the 맛있다/멋있다 compound pattern again

steps.note:
크다: to be big.
크다 → 커요 (polite present).

NO separate 'is' needed.
집이 커요: The house is-big.
크다 does BOTH jobs: adjective + copula.

작은 집이에요 ✗
집이 커요 ✓

작다: to be small.
작다 → 작아요 (polite present).

Opposite of 크다 (big).
작아요 = same conjugation: stem + 아/어요.

큰 집 ↔ 작은 집
(big house ↔ small house — before noun form)

예쁘다: to be pretty/beautiful.
예쁘다 → 예뻐요.

⚡ JRTF: 예쁘다 changes shape slightly.
예쁘 + 어요 → 예뻐요 (ㅡ drops before 어).
This is the 으-pattern, explained in Unit 9.

예쁜 꽃 (pretty flower — before noun form).

steps.exampleEn: My older brother is so cool!
steps.note:
COMPOUND: 멋 (style/cool) + 있다 (exist).
Literally: 'style exists' = cool/stylish.

Same structure as 맛있다!
맛 + 있다: taste exists = delicious
멋 + 있다: style exists = cool

멋있어요! = cool/stylish/impressive (all-purpose compliment).

귀엽다: to be cute.
귀여워요: is cute (polite).

⚡ JRTF: 귀엽다 → 귀여워요 (ㅂ-irregular).
Same pattern as 덥다 → 더워요.
Full explanation in Unit 7.

귀여운 아기 (cute baby — before noun form).

steps.title: 📐 Korean Adjectives vs English Adjectives
steps.text:
English:
adjective: 'big'
verb phrase: 'is big'
→ TWO different words doing TWO jobs

Korean:
크다 = big AND to-be-big
→ ONE word doing BOTH jobs

Conjugation test:
크다 → 커요 (present)
컸어요 (past — taught in U7)
클 거예요 (future — taught in U8)

Descriptive verbs conjugate exactly like action verbs.
The same endings. The same irregular patterns.
The same train structure.

This is why they're called 형용사 (descriptive verbs),
not 'adjectives' in Korean grammar.

steps.q: Korean adjectives are different from English because:
steps.hint: 크다 → 커요, like a verb

opts: I go to the big house
steps.hint: 크다 = to be big → 커요
귀엽다 → 귀여워요 (JRTF: ㅂ-irregular)
Style + exists = cool/stylish
예쁘다 → 예뻐요 (JRTF)
크다 → 커요

steps.s: 이 강아지가 ___! (This puppy is so cute!)
steps.hint: 귀엽다 → 귀여워요 (ㅂ-irregular JRTF)
The house is big. The puppy is cute. The flower is pretty.

steps.q: Which is the correct polite form of 작다?
steps.hint: 작 + 아요 (ㅏ vowel in stem → -아요)

steps.note:
나쁘다: to be bad.
나쁘다 → 나빠요.

좋다 (good) ↔ 나쁘다 (bad).

⚡ JRTF: 나쁘다 → 나빠요 (으-irregular like 예쁘다).
Full pattern in Unit 9.

steps.exampleEn: There are a lot of people. There is little money.
steps.note:
많다 → 많아요: there is a lot / many.
적다 → 적어요: there is little / few.

시간이 많아요: lots of time.
사람이 많아요: lots of people.
돈이 적어요: little money.

많다/적다: both regular.

steps.example: 이 버스가 느려요. 저 기차는 빨라요.
steps.exampleEn: This bus is slow. That train is fast.
steps.note:
느리다 → 느려요: slow.
빠르다 → 빨라요: fast.

느리다 ↔ 빠르다: slow ↔ fast.

⚡ JRTF: 빨라요 (르-irregular — Unit 9).
느려요 is regular.

steps.exampleEn: Korean is difficult. But it's interesting!
steps.note:
어렵다 → 어려워요: difficult.
쉽다 → 쉬워요: easy.

⚡ JRTF: both ㅂ-irregular (Unit 7).
어렵다 ↔ 쉽다: hard ↔ easy.

바쁘다: to be busy.
바쁘다 → 바빠요.

⚡ JRTF: 으-irregular like 예쁘다/나쁘다 (Unit 9).
요즘 많이 바빠요: I'm very busy lately.

steps.exampleEn: I don't like this. / I don't want this.
steps.note:
싫다: to dislike, to not want.
싫다 → 싫어요.

좋아요 (like) ↔ 싫어요 (dislike/don't want).

밥 싫어요: I don't want rice.
이거 싫어요: I don't like this.

steps.exampleEn: It's a new book. This bag is old.
steps.note:
새: new. Used DIRECTLY before nouns.
새 책: new book. 새 옷: new clothes.

오래되다: to be old (things, not people).
오래됐어요: it's (become) old.

새 사람: new person (새로운 사람 also fine).

재미없다 = 재미(fun) + 없다(doesn't exist).
'No fun exists' = boring.

재미있다 (interesting) ↔ 재미없다 (boring).
맛있다 (delicious) ↔ 맛없다 (tasteless).

Same compound logic: 없다 negates the compound.

steps.title: 🎨 Colors — The Full A1 Set
steps.text:
You know: 빨간 (red), 파란 (blue), 초록 (green), 하늘색 (sky blue).

Now add:
노랗다 → 노란 (yellow) ⚡ JRTF: ㅎ-irregular, Unit 11
하얗다 → 하얀 (white) ⚡ JRTF: ㅎ-irregular, Unit 11
검다 → 검은 (black) — regular
분홍색 (pink) | 보라색 (purple)

-색 (色) = color suffix: 노란색, 하얀색 all valid.

Full set:
빨간(색) red | 주황색 orange | 노란(색) yellow
초록(색) green | 파란(색) blue | 보라색 purple
분홍색 pink | 하얀(색) white | 검은(색) black | 하늘색 sky blue

steps.hint: 좋다 = good, 나쁘다 = bad

steps.q: 'There are a lot of people' — correct:
steps.hint: 많다 → 많아요: there is a lot
⚡ JRTF: 어렵다 → 어려워요 (ㅂ-irregular)
재미 (fun/interest) + 없다 (doesn't exist) = boring
새 goes directly before nouns: 새 책, 새 옷, 새 친구
재미없다 → 재미없어요

steps.s: 요즘 많이 ___. (I've been really busy lately)
steps.hint: 바쁘다 → 바빠요 (으-irregular JRTF)

steps.exampleEn: I'm happy when I'm with family.
steps.note:
행복하다: happy.
행복 (幸福): 행 (luck/fortune) + 복 (blessing).

하다 verb → 행복해요.

행복하다 ↔ 불행하다 (unhappy).
불 (不: not) + 행복 = unhappy.

슬프다: sad.
으-irregular: 슬프 + 어요 → ㅡ drops → 슬퍼요.

슬프다 ↔ 기쁘다 (glad/joyful).
기쁘다 → 기뻐요 (same 으-irregular pattern).

중요하다: important.
중요 (重要): 중 (heavy/weighty) + 요 (essential).

하다 verb → 중요해요.
건강이 중요해요: health is important.
이것은 중요해요: this is important.

깨끗하다: clean.
하다 verb → 깨끗해요.

깨끗하다 ↔ 더럽다 (dirty).
더럽다 → 더러워요 (ㅂ-irregular).

방이 깨끗해요: the room is clean.

편하다: comfortable/convenient.
불편하다: uncomfortable/inconvenient.

편 (便: convenience) + 하다.
불 (不: not) + 편하다 = uncomfortable.

이 신발이 편해요: these shoes are comfortable.
지하철이 불편해요: the subway is inconvenient.

높다: high/tall (things, not people).
낮다: low/short.

높다 → 높아요 (regular).
낮다 → 낮아요 (regular).

산이 높아요: the mountain is high.
온도가 낮아요: the temperature is low.

다르다: different.
르-irregular: 다르 + 아요 → 달라요.

Same pattern as 모르다 → 몰라요.

같다 (same) ↔ 다르다 (different).

멀다: far. ㄹ-stem.
멀다 → 멀어요 (regular: ㄹ stays before vowel).

가깝다: close/near. ㅂ-irregular.
가깝다 → 가까워요 (ㅂ→우).

집에서 학교까지 멀어요?: Is it far from home to school?
아니요, 가까워요: No, it's close.

무섭다: scary/frightening.
ㅂ-irregular: 무섭 → 무서우 + 어요 = 무서워요.

무서워요 can mean:
① That thing is scary (그 영화가 무서워요)
② I'm scared (저는 무서워요)

steps.hint: 행 (幸) = luck/fortune. 복 (福) = blessing. 행복 = fortunate blessing = happiness.
ㅡ drops. 슬프 → 슬퍼요.
ㅂ→우: 가깝→가까우+어요 = 가까워요.

steps.note:
싫어하다: to dislike/hate.
싫다 (disliked/hateful) + 어하다 (externalized form).

좋아하다 ↔ 싫어하다: like ↔ dislike.

저는 매운 음식을 싫어해요: I dislike spicy food.
그 사람을 싫어해요: I dislike that person.

싫다 → 싫어요: I don't want it / I don't like it (feeling).
싫어하다 → 싫어해요: to dislike (action/behavior).

steps.title: P26 Moment: You've Been Using These Since Day One
steps.desc: 은/는, 이/가, 을/를. You've seen all three in every unit since U1. Today they get their names, their functions, and their differences. This is the most important grammar lesson in A1.
goals: Name and distinguish 은/는, 이/가, 을/를
Understand topic vs subject vs object
Own the particle system permanently

steps.exampleEn: As for me, I am a student.
steps.note:
은/는: TOPIC marker.

'As for X...' — sets what the sentence is about.

은 after consonant: 학생은, 밥은
는 after vowel: 저는, 커피는

저는 학생이에요:
'As for me (topic) — I am a student.'

Use 은/는 for: context, contrast, known info.

steps.exampleEn: There is coffee. (coffee is the thing that exists)
steps.note:
이/가: SUBJECT marker.

Marks the doer OR the thing the predicate describes.
Used for NEW information or EMPHASIS.

이 after consonant: 학생이, 시간이
가 after vowel: 커피가, 저가

커피가 있어요:
'Coffee (subject, new info) — exists.'

steps.exampleEn: I eat rice. (rice = what is eaten)
steps.note:
을/를: OBJECT marker.

Marks WHAT the verb acts on.

을 after consonant: 밥을, 책을, 라면을
를 after vowel: 커피를, 물을 ✗ → 물을 ✓ (물 ends in ㄹ = consonant → 을)

밥을 먹어요:
Rice (object) — eat.

steps.title: ⭐ The Three Particles: Full System
steps.text:
은/는 = TOPIC — As for X... (context/contrast)
이/가 = SUBJECT — New info / doer / emphasis
을/를 = OBJECT — What the verb acts upon

Every sentence you know follows this system:

저는 커피를 마셔요.
→ As for me, I drink coffee. (저는 = topic, 커피를 = object)

커피가 있어요.
→ There is coffee. (커피가 = subject, new info)

학교에서 공부해요.
→ (I) study at school. (topic omitted — understood from context)
→ '(I) study at school.'

steps.title: 🧩 은/는 vs 이/가: The Hard Part
steps.text:
은/는 = TOPIC (known info, contrast, context)
이/가 = SUBJECT (new info, emphasis, focus)

저는 학생이에요.
→ As for me — I'm a student. (general, topic)

제가 학생이에요.
→ It's ME who is the student. (emphasis, subject)

한국어는 재미있어요.
→ As for Korean — it's interesting. (topic)

한국어가 재미있어요.
→ Korean is what's interesting. (focused subject — new info)

For beginners: default to 은/는 for 'I/me' in statements.
Use 이/가 when presenting new information.

steps.hint: 'As for X...' — sets the topic
What the verb acts upon
New information or emphasis

steps.q: '저는 커피를 마셔요.' Which particle marks the topic?
steps.hint: 는 = topic marker. 저는 = 'as for me...'

opts: Subject (coffee is the thing that exists)
steps.ans: Subject (coffee is the thing that exists)
steps.hint: 이/가 marks the subject — the thing doing/being the predicate
저는 (topic) 물을 (object: 물 ends in consonant ㄹ → 을) 마셔요
저 ends in vowel → topic marker 는
커피 ends in vowel → subject marker 가

steps.s: 저는 밥___ 먹어요. (I eat rice — rice is object)
steps.hint: 밥 ends in consonant ㅂ → object marker 을
I (topic) study Korean (object). Korean (subject) is interesting.

steps.q: 제가 학생이에요 vs 저는 학생이에요: the difference is:
opts: 제가 emphasizes IT'S ME, 저는 states a fact
steps.ans: 제가 emphasizes IT'S ME, 저는 states a fact
steps.hint: 이/가 adds emphasis: 'it's ME who is the student'

steps.q: 도 replaces 은/는 or 이/가. It means:
steps.hint: 저도 = me too. 도 replaces 는 or 가 entirely.

steps.title: Big House, Cute Dog, Pretty Flower
steps.desc: When Korean adjectives go BEFORE a noun, they change form. This is the -ㄴ/은 modifier. You'll see the pattern now — Unit 7 gives it its full name.
goals: Use adjective + noun patterns
Learn the modifier form through examples
Recognize the pattern without memorizing rules yet

steps.exampleEn: I live in a big house.
steps.note:
크다 (to be big) → 큰 (before a noun).

크다 → 커요 (predicate)
크다 → 큰 [noun] (modifier)

큰 집: big house
큰 가방: big bag
큰 목소리: loud voice (literally: big voice)

작다 (to be small) → 작은 (before a noun).

작다 → 작아요 (predicate)
작다 → 작은 [noun] (modifier)

작은 강아지: small puppy
작은 가방: small bag
작은 방: small room

steps.exampleEn: I want to buy pretty flowers.
steps.note:
예쁘다 (to be pretty) → 예쁜 (before a noun).

예쁘다 → 예뻐요 (predicate)
예쁘다 → 예쁜 [noun] (modifier)

예쁜 꽃: pretty flower
예쁜 사람: pretty person

steps.exampleEn: I want to eat delicious food.
steps.note:
맛있다 → 맛있는 (before a noun).

맛있다 ends in 있다 → use -는 before nouns (not -은/ㄴ).

맛있는 음식 ✓ (not 맛있은 음식)

맛있는 음식: delicious food
멋있는 사람: cool/stylish person
재미있는 영화: interesting movie

steps.title: 🏷️ The Modifier Pattern: Preview
steps.text:
You've just seen two modifier forms:

-ㄴ/은: used with most descriptive verbs
크다 → 큰, 작다 → 작은, 예쁘다 → 예쁜

-는: used with 있다/없다 compounds
맛있다 → 맛있는, 재미있다 → 재미있는

The full rules come in Unit 7.
For now: just RECOGNIZE these patterns.

English: adjective → adjective (same form)
'big' → 'big house'

Korean: adjective → modifier form → noun
크다 → 큰 → 큰 집

The change is always predictable.
You'll see the pattern many times before U7.

steps.hint: 크다 → 큰 (modifier form before nouns)
예쁘다 → 예쁜 (modifier form)
있다 compounds use -는 before nouns
큰 = big (modifier), 목소리 = voice. 'Big voice' = loud voice.

steps.s: ___ 강아지가 귀여워요. (The small puppy is cute — modifier form)
steps.hint: 작다 → 작은 before a noun
멋있다 → 멋있는 (있다 compound → -는 modifier)

steps.s: 저는 ___ 집에 살고 싶어요. (I want to live in a big house)
steps.hint: 크다 → 큰 (modifier before noun)
The small puppy went to the pretty person.

steps.q: 재미있는 영화 = 재미있다 + 는 + 영화. 재미있다 means:
steps.hint: 재미 (fun/interest) + 있다 (exists) = interesting

steps.title: What Holds Korean Families — and Society — Together
steps.desc: 정 (jeong) is the deep bond that Korean culture is built on. No English translation exists. Understanding 정 unlocks Korean social behavior: why Koreans share food with strangers, why age hierarchy governs every interaction, why 우리 means 'my'.
goals: Understand 정 as a cultural concept
Know how age hierarchy works in families
Use 몇 살이에요? and 연세가 어떻게 되세요?

steps.exampleEn: I've grown deeply attached. (Jeong has entered.)
steps.note:
정 = accumulated warmth between people who share life.
Deeper than love, loyalty, or friendship.
Built through: eating together, shared hardship, small daily kindnesses over years.

정이 들다.
→ Jeong has formed. (you've bonded deeply)

정이 없다.
→ No jeong. (worst thing to say about someone)

우리 엄마, 우리 집, 우리 나라.
→ All said 우리 (our) because everything is shared.

jeong has entered (you've bonded deeply)
정이 없다: no jeong — the worst thing to say about someone

우리 엄마, 우리 집, 우리 나라:
All reflect 정 — we share everything together.

나이: age.
살: age counter (native Korean).
몇 살이에요?: 'How old are you?' (casual-polite)

저는 스물세 살이에요: I am 23 years old.
스물(20) + 세(3) = 23 살

Age uses NATIVE Korean numbers for 1–99.
스물: 20 (native), 서른: 30, 마흔: 40...

연세: respectful word for 'age' (used for elders).
나이 → 연세 (honorific upgrade).

어떻게 되세요?: respectful 'how is it?'

Rule: NEVER ask an elder's age with 몇 살이에요.
Always use 연세가 어떻게 되세요?

steps.title: 🧓 Age Hierarchy: How It Works
steps.text:
Korean society runs on age hierarchy.
Every relationship has a clear elder/younger structure.

Why it matters linguistically:
• Speech level changes based on age
• Address terms change (형, 오빠, 선생님, 씨...)
• Who eats first, who speaks first, who pays

Practical rules:

1. Ask age early — Koreans ask quickly, it's not rude.
   But USE the right question by context:
   몇 살이에요? = peers / casual
   연세가 어떻게 되세요? = elders / formal

2. If they're older: use -요 endings always.
   If younger: you can use casual speech (Unit 10).

3. The honorific system (존댓말 / 반말) emerges from this.
   You'll learn it fully in Unit 9-10.

정 + age hierarchy = Korean social fabric.

opts: Deep bond built through shared life over time
steps.ans: Deep bond built through shared life over time
steps.hint: 정 is accumulated through shared meals, hardship, daily kindness
Use 연세가 어떻게 되세요? for elders

steps.q: Age in Korean uses which number system?
steps.hint: 살 counter uses native Korean: 스물 살, 서른 살
정이 없다 is a harsh criticism of someone's character

steps.s: 저는 스물___ 살이에요. (I am 25 years old)
steps.hint: 25 = 스물(20) + 다섯(5). Native Korean numbers.

steps.s: 어르신께 나이를 여쭐 때: '___가 어떻게 되세요?'
steps.hint: Honorific word for age = 연세
I am 23 years old. How old are you?

steps.q: Koreans ask age early in conversations because:
opts: Age determines speech level and relationship terms
steps.ans: Age determines speech level and relationship terms
steps.hint: Age → 형/오빠/언니 etc. → speech level → entire social framework

steps.note:
마음: heart/mind/feelings.

Pure Korean word (not Sino-Korean).

마음이 따뜻해요: warm-hearted.
마음이 아파요: my heart hurts.
마음에 들다: to like (lit: enter the heart).

정 (deep bond) lives in the 마음.
한국인의 마음: the Korean heart.

steps.title: Unit 5 Complete
steps.desc: Family vocabulary, adjective verbs, the particle trio named, modifier forms, and Korean cultural depth. Everything from U1–U5 vocabulary.
goals: Verify particle system mastery
Test family vocabulary
Confirm adjective verb understanding

steps.exampleEn: My family is big. Mom is pretty.
steps.note:
FAMILY: full tree from 할아버지 to 여동생.
SIBLINGS: 형/오빠, 누나/언니 (speaker gender matters).
PARTICLES NAMED: 은/는 topic, 이/가 subject, 을/를 object.
ADJECTIVES ARE VERBS: 크다→커요, 작다→작아요.
MODIFIER FORM: 큰 집, 작은 방, 예쁜 꽃.
우리: my/our for family + close things.

steps.q: 은/는 vs 이/가 vs 을/를 — in order:
steps.hint: 은/는 = topic, 이/가 = subject, 을/를 = object
할 = grand prefix

opts: ARE verbs and conjugate the same way
steps.ans: ARE verbs and conjugate the same way
steps.hint: 크다 → 커요, exactly like an action verb
Korean collectivism: 우리 is used for family members
Style + exists = cool/stylish
예쁘다 → 예쁜 (modifier form before nouns)

steps.q: Asking an elderly person's age — correct form:
steps.hint: 연세 = honorific word for age
First blank: 저 (vowel ending) + topic marker
커피 ends in vowel → object marker 를

steps.s: ___ 강아지가 귀여워요. (The small puppy is cute)
steps.hint: 작다 → 작은 (modifier form before noun)
I (topic) study Korean (object). Korean (subject/new info) is interesting.
정이 들다 = jeong has entered = you've bonded deeply
